title: Pinicking by Elk River Dam; Japanese in Elk River
description: Picnicing by the dam at Elk River. Japanese at Elk River were very nice but didn't mix.

title: A mean teacher; sister's death; accidents at home; closeness of town children
description: A mean teacher. Jockheck home. Little sister's death from non-contagious meningitis. Her sister had a sledding accident, and two other accidents. The closeness of children in the town has lasted through their lifetimes.

title: Skating in the winter and husband suprising townspeople with skating
description: Skating in winter. Husband astounded the townspeople by his skating when he arrived from Canada.

title: Taking college prep classes at Elk River School; no fear of working men; getting candy for delivering meat
description: College preparatory work was required at Elk River school. Town buildings. Parents had no fear for their children from the working men; candy for delivering meat.

title: Where father learned butchering trade; how they came to the area; working in the meat market; Christmas celebrations
description: Father learned the butchering trade in Germany. He came to Palouse from Denver for his health, and moved to Elk River in 1912 for the opportunity. Mother was raised in Denver. Operating the meat market. The children had to help grind meat, pick chickens, and deliver meat; they worked too hard. Brother Fred lost two fingers in the grinder on July fourth. For throwing sawdust in pickles and meat, they got no presents from Santa Claus. Christmas celebration.

title: Purchases for the children; childhood freedom; Easter celebrations
description: Father never objected to mother's purchases for children. Easter eggs. Father didn't share responsibility with mother, though she was a better manager than he was. The children had less freedom because of 4-ather's attitude about being a businessman.

title: How and what they played growing up; games; what boyfriends were called
description: Children's play in the woods. Playhouses. Punishment for misusing brother's bicycle. Sledding on the hills. Home parties and birthdays. Pom Pom Pullaway. Kids played in their own neighborhoods. Playing under the church ramp. Boyfriends were calledTellowsV

title: Young people dating; dancing; husband was an expert violinist
description: Young people dating walked on the railroad tracks. Father disapproved of her dancing, but she started to at fifteen. Local bands. Husband an expert violinist; a man who didn't like him derided his playing.

title: How the world wars affected her German family; school sports
description: The world wars were difficult for Germans. In the First World War father's meat went bad because he could only sell it on certain days. Germans had to be careful. One teacher was mean to her, but she didn't know of people being mean to her parents. Yardage from Germany stopped during the war. School sports.

title: Separate social lives for cultural groups; local fire fighting practices; community events and social life
description: IWW's in jail. Foreign families had their own social life. An Italian quartet. Andrew Bloom. Munson told men in poolhall to fight fire. On two occasions trains were readied to evacuate people from Elk River; mother's confidence in face of fire. Community picnics. Lack of participation by single men in community social affairs.

title: Social activities for married couples and wives; going to basketball games by train
description: Social activities for married couples and wives. Train transportation to other towns for basketball games; this was a chance to make friends.

title: When the mill left Elk River and its affects on the town; sending care packages to Germany after World War II; visits to Denver
description: Taking the mill out of Elk River - regrets of Potlatch in retrospect; hard on the people of the town. Difficulties in father's business - his leniency with credit, effect of automobiles. His meat market became a grocery store. Despite parents' generous donations to charity, others did not offer to help German children after the Second World War. Her packages to Germany after the Second War. Trains to Denver to visit mother's parents. She and her brother were "bad" children.

title: working at Elk River print shop for William Marineau; trying to into the US; why he left Colfax
description: (J. Les Clark: ) Going to work at the Elk River print shop for William Marineau. Marineau promised his employment to an immigration investigator. He was turned away trying to get into the U.S. because they thought he was too young to be a printer. He left Colfax after less than a week because the Ku Klux Klan was burning crosses for his boss, a Catholic. Dealing with immigration.

title: Learning to print in Trail; wages of printing; working in Elk River
description: Learning to print in Trail for four years. Wages of $22. 50 for 48 hours. Work in Elk River. Apprenticing was rough; you had tears in your eyes.

title: Elk River Falls; enjoyment of living in a small town
description: Elk River Falls. His enjoyment of Elk River, where everyone knew everyone; the men from the mill ribbed each other in the poolhalls.

title: Union thinking he'd do better printing in large city; playing sports in Elk River; coating requests; speech patterns
description: Union secretary thought he would do better to print in a city. He played sports in Elk River. He turned down a request from coach of Portland Buckaroos to play hockey on the Rosslyn farm team because it was near Trail. He got ribbed because of the way he talked.

title: Soliciting printing jobs around Elk River and surrounding area; press breakdown; History of Elk River Press; Linotype versus Simplex printing
description: Marineau was smart to move to Moscow and work for Dr. Robinson. At Elk River he went around the country and solicited a great deal of job printing. The paper took them only two days a week. A press breakdown. Small town presses depended on job printing. Marineau had good job printing equipment. History of Elk River press. Advantage of linotype over Simplex.

title: Music in Elk River; Courtship of wife; Early women graduates from Elk River; new women married quickly in Elk River
description: Elk River band. Foreigners in town. Her picture. Their courtship took five years. She went to Lewiston Normal with his moral support. She wanted and expected to live away from Elk River; he was the one eligible man who didn't work in the mill. Early women graduates from Elk River. Women who came to Elk River to work often married rather quickly.

title: Playing amateur hockey in Portland; history of mill and timber in Elk River
description: He became an amateur hockey star in Portland, and got a job with the team sponsor. His belief that the one-industry town was a poor risk. Marineau used to emphasize that there was eighty years of timber at Elk River. For a brief time the Potlatch company considered shipping the timber to Elk River and Weippe rather than Lewiston.

title: Marineau's excitment about mill expansion and problems associated with it; Potlatch camps on Breakfast Creek
description: Marineau's excitement about prospects for expansion. Heavy winter snows a drawback. Another consideration may have been that Potlatch's twenty year contract with The Milwaukee was expiring, and the railroad may have planned to raise the rates. Potlatch men camped on Breakfast Creek, which may have increased their interest in the route to Lewiston.

title: Affects of the Depression on Portland printing; Working at printing in Eugene, Springfield and linotype school in San Francisco
description: Depression hit Portland printing hard after 1929 stock crash. The job from playing hockey. Working in Eugene and Springfield. Decision to go to linotype school in San Francisco.

title: Death of Lamphere and what it ment for Dr. Robinson; Moscow newspaper buys out Troy newspaper
description: If Lamphere hadn't died, he would have kept up the fight against Dr. Robinson. Marineau noted that Robinson's materials sold well. The Moscow News-Review started by buying out the Troy News so that they could run legal notice without a 72 week delay.

title: Opposition to school consolidation; pressure against paper
description: His opposition to school consolidation as he ran Nevada City newspaper in the fifties, believing it would kill the town. He kept winning at the elections until his paper was bought out. Pressures against his paper.

title: Community church was the center of activities; Shut down of Elk River movie theater on sundays; Klan presence in the area; Helmer dance hall
description: Community church was center of local activity. As a youngster she got afraid of the angry preaching. The Catholic Church did not encourage the foreigners to attend. Elk River shut down the movie theatre on Sundays at the instigation of the church; this drove the youth out of town and out of church. The leader of the movement was in the Klan. The Klan didn't scare people and wasn't too strong. Colfax had a large number of Catholics. Helmer dance pavilion was very popular - Lawrence said he started it because people would pay for entertainment better than groceries.

title: Deaths of Emil Anderson and Malker Anderson
description: Emil Anderson may have died accidentally, because the fuse may have gone too fast. Death of Malker Anderson.

title: Torgerson dynasty at Park; Malker Anderson's wife; Southern Idaho connections; girls meeting husbands at cmaps
description: Torgerson dynasty at Park - he employed many people for very low wages. Malker's strange wife. Axel Anderson's wife was a hard worker and a reader; her father was Mr. Hontz, a noted artist from south Idaho. Many girls met their husbands by flunkeying in the camps.

title: Elk River culture; music in Elk River; Pool halls and attendance at them
description: Elk River had a lot of culture - music and singing, plays (put on by Ladies' Aid). Women used calling cards on visits. People who attended the Catholic Church. Ed Allen's pool hall and confectionary; attendance at various pool halls.

title: Tried to stop the Elk River movie theater from shutting down on Sundays; church alienating youn people
description: Bill Marineau voted against shutting down the movie theatre on Sunday on the city council, but was outvoted. This was a turning point, the church alienating the young people.

title: Elk River plays and theatres; Neva Hill settlement; Elk River Hospital; playing games
description: Plays and theatres. At a trick-or-treat party all the kids got to eat were soda crackers as a trick. Neva Hill had a settlement. The Elk River hospital was complete for a small town. Playing the Italian ball-throwing game.

title: Tramp printers going where the work was; working cards; brotherhood in the printing union declining
description: Noted tramp printers. They went where the work was, and could pass their union card for a little money. Travelling and working cards. They drank and were highly skilled. Brotherhood in the printing union has declined.
